metadata.title: Physics theory graph (gold, theory-focused revision)
metadata.source_note: Rewritten from the uploaded physics graph to reduce hub over-dependence, remove vague relations, and sharpen contradiction/bridge structure.

relation_convention.contradict: false or opposing claim -> target claim

notes: Summary nodes are structural hubs only.
Ordinary claims are not attached to hubs by default; cluster membership lives in metadata.
Misconceptions are encoded in metadata rather than special node types.
Weak labels such as related_to were removed.
Part_of edges were reduced to hub hierarchy only.
Gold revision adds explicit theory nodes, law-level bridges, and component summaries for training supervision.

nodes.text: A battery supplies a fixed current regardless of circuit resistance.
Bridge insights connect major physics clusters through shared reasoning patterns.
A changing magnetic field can induce an electromotive force and drive current.
Electric charges create electric fields.
Coulomb's law states that the electric force between two point charges is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation.
Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge.
A double-slit pattern is a standard example of wave interference.
An isolated elastic collision is a standard example where total momentum is conserved.
An electric field exerts force on a charged particle.
Electrical power in a circuit can be expressed as P = IV, relating energy transfer rate to current and potential difference.
Electromagnetic theory links electric fields, induction, and light.
Electromagnetic waves can propagate through a vacuum and do not require a material medium.
Electromagnetic theory explains how charges create fields, fields do work on charge, circuits encode those effects through voltage and current, and changing magnetic flux induces emf.
Electromagnetism studies charge, fields, circuits, and induction.
Energy conservation appears in both mechanics and thermodynamics.
Thermodynamic reasoning combines energy accounting with directionality: the first law tracks conservation, while the second law distinguishes which energy transfers are spontaneous.
Entropy tends to increase in an isolated system.
Faraday's law states that the induced electromotive force is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through a circuit.

Electromagnetism connects fields, potential differences, currents, and power: fields move charge, potential measures energy per charge, and circuits translate those ideas into measurable current and power.
The first law of thermodynamics states that energy is conserved as heat and work are exchanged.
Mechanics unifies force laws, momentum change, and work-energy reasoning: the same dynamical interaction can be analyzed through acceleration, impulse, or energy transfer depending on the question.
When light refracts into a new medium, its frequency changes while its wavelength stays fixed.
Heat is energy transferred because of a temperature difference.
Heat and temperature are the same physical quantity.
Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects in a vacuum.
The ideal gas law relates pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of gas through PV = nRT.
Impulse equals the change in momentum, so a net force acting over a time interval changes a system's momentum.
Waves can interfere constructively or destructively through superposition.
Internal energy is a state function, so its change depends only on the initial and final thermodynamic states, not on the path taken between them.
Lenz's law states that an induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
Mechanical energy is conserved when only conservative forces do work.
Mechanics links net force, momentum change, collision reasoning, and work-energy ideas into one coherent framework for predicting motion.
Mechanics studies force, motion, momentum, and mechanical energy.
Wave behavior depends on how a medium responds: refraction, propagation speed, and the ability of sound to travel all reflect the physical properties of the medium.
Total momentum is conserved in an isolated system.
Momentum equals mass times velocity.
The net force on an object is the vector sum of all individual forces acting on it.
Newton's second law states that net force equals mass times acceleration.
For a fixed resistance, current is proportional to voltage.
Physics studies matter, energy, motion, fields, and waves.
Across physics, conserved quantities, field descriptions, and medium-dependent propagation patterns reveal common reasoning structures that tie separate subfields together.
Potential difference is work per unit charge between two points.
A prism is a standard example of refraction of visible light.
Refraction occurs because a wave changes speed when it enters a different medium.
The second law of thermodynamics states that isolated systems evolve toward states of greater total entropy and that spontaneous processes have a preferred direction.
Snell's law relates angles of incidence and refraction to the wave speeds or refractive indices of two media.
Sound waves require a material medium to propagate.
Sound waves can travel through a vacuum.
Spontaneous heat flow from hotter to colder regions increases total entropy.
When waves overlap, the resulting displacement is the sum of the individual displacements; this is the superposition principle.
Temperature tracks the average kinetic energy of particles in a system.
Thermodynamics combines energy bookkeeping, microscopic interpretations of temperature, the meaning of heat transfer, and entropy-based directionality into one theory of macroscopic change.
Thermodynamics studies heat, temperature, entropy, and energy transfer.
Near Earth, objects in a vacuum fall with the same gravitational acceleration regardless of mass.
Visible light is an electromagnetic wave.
When a wave enters a new medium, its frequency is fixed by the source, while speed and wavelength may change.
Comparing sound and light reveals a deep wave distinction: mechanical waves require matter to oscillate, while electromagnetic waves can self-propagate through vacuum.
Wave speed equals frequency times wavelength.
Wave theory organizes speed, wavelength, frequency, superposition, interference, refraction, and medium dependence into a single account of propagation and optical behavior.
Wave physics studies propagation, interference, sound, and light.
The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy.
With zero net force, an object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity.

metadata.note: Both belong to the same electromagnetic framework.
Mechanical-energy conservation is a narrower conservation statement than the thermodynamic energy balance.
Zero external impulse yields momentum conservation.
Conservative-force energy conservation is a special work-energy structure.
Ideal-gas processes are often analyzed through thermodynamic energy relations.
